text: Cornelius was one of those Gentiles who, without adopting the rite of circumcision, conformed to the Jewish religion in general; he was not a proselyte in the full sense.
The last ten words of this verse are omitted in the Greek manuscripts.
St Peter seems to have interpreted the command as a direction to satisfy his hunger indiscriminately, although some of the creatures he saw were unclean according to the Mosaic law.
Some manuscripts imply that Cornelius was fasting until three in the afternoon, the time of his vision.
Some manuscripts add, at the end of this verse, ‘and he, when he comes, will speak to thee’.

This is the only occasion on which we hear of the Holy Spirit being granted to those who, although they had the desire of baptism, had not yet received that sacrament. It seems that the early Church needed special encouragement before it adopted the practice of receiving as converts those who did not conform to the full law of Moses.
